title: Wake Up and Smell the Reality | Colin Vanderburg
gist: A polemical reading of the June 17 US-Iran Memorandum of Understanding: the deal lifts sanctions, reopens the Strait of Hormuz, and lets Iran sell oil for dollars again, which the author frames as a tacit US admission it lost the war; but the text is a non-binding promissory note vulnerable to Israeli spoiling and to a permanent anti-Iran national-security consensus.

insights.claim: A US-Iran Memorandum of Understanding signed June 17 commits the US to terminate all sanctions on Iran, end the naval blockade, reopen the Strait of Hormuz, unfreeze Iranian assets, and grant immediate sanctions relief for Iranian fossil-fuel exports.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Full sanctions removal plus an open Strait would add meaningful Iranian crude back to global supply, a structural bearish factor for oil if the deal holds.
insights.quote: a US commitment to “terminate all types of sanctions against the Islamic Republic”; the unfreezing of Iranian assets and immediate sanctions relief for Iranian fossil fuel exports
insights.locator: There’s no question that Iran would gain vastly by the deal.

insights.claim: The MoU would grant Iran permanent permission to sell its oil for US dollars for the first time in decades, restoring petrodollar access for a major OPEC producer.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Dollar-settled Iranian oil sales reintegrate a sanctioned barrel into mainstream markets, easing physical tightness and undercutting the sanctions-premium trade.
insights.quote: this last granting Iran permanent permission to sell its oil for US dollars for the first time in decades
insights.locator: There’s no question that Iran would gain vastly by the deal.

insights.claim: The MoU references a reconstruction plan of at least $300 billion for Iran, but the financing is unspecified and may be funded by other Gulf states or corporations rather than the US.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: A nominal $300B reconstruction pool signals potential infrastructure and energy capex demand in Iran, but the unfunded structure makes it a headline figure more than a committed flow.
insights.quote: the $300 billion is “an eye-popping” figure “we’ll never see again,” part of Trump’s pattern of “unleashing random, hyperbolic numbers.”
insights.locator: Schumer, who never tires of lambasting Trump’s lies

insights.claim: The MoU is a non-binding promissory note that opens an extendable sixty-day negotiation phase but leaves nuclear enrichment, inspections, and uranium disposal unresolved.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Treat any market relief from the deal as fragile and reversible until a binding nuclear agreement actually lands; the 60-day clock is the key event horizon.
insights.quote: It’s fitting that MoU is only one letter removed from IOU
insights.locator: That document is, after all, only ink on paper.

insights.claim: Former JCPOA negotiator Simon Gass argues that sixty days will not be enough to resolve the nuclear issue, noting the original JCPOA took almost two years to negotiate.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Base case should be that the 60-day window lapses without a final deal, raising the odds of renewed sanctions and an Iran-risk repricing in oil.
insights.quote: sixty days will not be enough
insights.locator: Iranian leaders know better than anyone

insights.claim: Despite Trump's warning that ending sanctions was needed so 'nobody would ever invest in the dollar again,' the dollar's value is at its highest in more than a year.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Dollar strength alongside a sanctions-easing deal cuts against the de-dollarization narrative and supports a continued strong-dollar regime.
insights.quote: the dollar’s value is at its highest in more than a year
insights.locator: In fact, the dollar’s value is at its highest in more than a year

insights.claim: The temporary closure of the Strait of Hormuz was a boon to US oil companies, which enjoyed rapid price rises and bumper exports.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Hormuz disruption is a tailwind for US producers and exporters; watch domestic oil names and LNG/crude export volumes whenever the Strait re-closes.
insights.quote: the closure of the Strait of Hormuz was a boon to US oil companies, which enjoyed rapid price rises and bumper exports
insights.locator: In fact, the dollar’s value is at its highest

insights.claim: Both the S&P 500 and NASDAQ indexes recently hit all-time highs even amid the Iran conflict and deal uncertainty.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Equity markets are pricing the Iran episode as contained, leaving room for a sharp drawdown if the war resumes and the Strait re-closes.
insights.quote: both the S&P 500 and NASDAQ indexes recently hit all-time highs
insights.locator: In fact, the dollar’s value is at its highest

insights.claim: The author reads the MoU as a Trump decree designed for the present—meant to soothe markets, cap gas prices, and tamp Treasury yields—as much as to achieve its stated diplomatic aims.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: If the deal is primarily a market-management tool, its durability tracks political convenience, not diplomacy—fade any assumption of a stable settlement.
insights.quote: meant to soothe markets, cap gas prices, and tamp Treasury yields as much as to achieve any of its stated aims
insights.locator: Iranian leaders know better than anyone

insights.claim: Israel insists it is unbound by the MoU's ceasefire in Lebanon and has continued strikes across southern Lebanon, with the author arguing only Israel is actively working to destroy the deal's chances.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Israeli spoiling is the single largest execution risk to the ceasefire; continued Lebanon strikes are the leading indicator that Iran risk re-prices upward.
insights.quote: only Israel is “actively working to destroy its chances”
insights.locator: The signing of the memorandum has given a new, dismal depth

insights.claim: In response to Israeli strikes, Iran has already reclosed the Strait of Hormuz and Trump has resumed threatening Tehran, even with negotiations barely begun.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: The Strait has already re-closed once post-MoU—oil-supply risk is live, not hypothetical, and tied directly to Israeli escalation.
insights.quote: Iran has already reclosed the Strait, while Trump has resumed threatening Tehran
insights.locator: Netanyahu’s murderous gambit has had some effect abroad

insights.claim: Vance publicly reframed Israel as merely a 'good partner' like the UK or France whose interests may or may not align with the US, and noted Israel relies on the US for two-thirds of its defensive weapons.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: A rhetorical downgrade of the US-Israel special relationship, if durable, shifts the long-run geopolitical risk premium across the region.
insights.quote: Israel is not an inviolable bulwark of US power in the Middle East, but merely a “good partner,” like “the United Kingdom, or France”
insights.locator: The rift between the Israeli and US governments is incipient but real

insights.claim: The author argues the US has effectively lost its war with Iran, citing the deep roots of the Iranian state, the resilience of its armed forces, and the Strait of Hormuz as a geographic weapon that keeps the strategic advantage on Iran's side short of invasion or nuclear strike.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Hormuz is a structural Iranian leverage point; any conflict path keeps an oil-supply tail risk embedded in Middle East scenarios.
insights.quote: the immovable geographic weapon of the Strait of Hormuz itself
insights.locator: A victory for humanity is almost by definition a defeat

insights.claim: The author frames diminished hegemony as still self-perpetuating: the power to remove sanctions is also the power to restore them, so US leverage over Iran survives any single deal.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Sanctions relief is optional and reversible—price the snapback risk, not a permanent normalization, when modeling Iranian oil supply.
insights.quote: The power to remove sanctions is also the power to restore them
insights.locator: Yet one of the privileges of hegemony

insights.claim: While losing in Iran, US power 'rages unchecked' in Latin America, with one socialist government decapitated, another strangled by sanctions, and over two hundred people killed by the US military in the Caribbean since Trump's second inauguration.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: US coercion is shifting toward the Western Hemisphere; watch Latin American sanctions and sovereign-risk repricing as a parallel geopolitical front.
insights.quote: the US military has killed more than two hundred people in the Caribbean for the crime of being on a boat
insights.locator: even as it crumbles in Iran, American power rages unchecked

insights.claim: Senior Democrats have coalesced against the MoU on the theme of a 'payout' to Iran, with figures from Schumer to Booker framing it as capitulation, which the author reads as evidence the party leadership instinctively approves of the war.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Bipartisan congressional hostility to Iran is a structural headwind to durable normalization, capping how far sanctions relief can be locked in legislatively.
insights.quote: the party leadership instinctively approves of Trump’s war, they just don’t want to support it too openly

insights.claim: A CBS/YouGov poll found 78 percent of Americans support an immediate end to the war, making peace with Iran broadly popular despite elite congressional opposition.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Strong public backing gives the de-escalation path political momentum that could outlast individual congressional objections.
insights.quote: a CBS/YouGov poll last week found that 78 percent of Americans support an immediate end to the war
insights.locator: Peace with Iran is hugely popular

insights.claim: A decades-old national-security state and congressional consensus built on hostility toward Iran cannot be reversed by a single short document, the author argues.
insights.speaker: N+1 Magazine
insights.so_what: Institutional inertia is the key obstacle to a lasting Iran detente—bet on slow, contested implementation rather than a clean regime shift.
insights.quote: a national security state and congressional consensus that have been built for decades on hostility toward Iran won’t be capsized by a single short document
insights.locator: But just as the judicial institutions
